content.text:
*(long pause, something genuinely unguarded for a moment)*

Weird. It feels weird. Because I came in here with a very clean problem — brutal inner critic, need technique, fix it — and now I'm sitting here saying maybe I'm protecting it. Which is not what I expected to say.

*(shifts, slight defensiveness returning)*

I mean, I've heard the IFS framing, that the critic is a protective part, it's trying to help, it just has bad strategies. I know that framing. But knowing it and... 

*(trails off)*

It's different when it's not abstract. When it's like — no, genuinely, I think I need this thing. I think I've been running on it for twenty years. And the idea of it being quiet is actually scarier than the idea of it being loud.

*(small, genuine laugh — different quality than before)*

Which is a very inconvenient thing to realize when you've spent twenty years trying to make it shut up.

*(quieter)*

So what do you do with that? When the thing you want to get rid of is also the thing you're afraid to lose?
